---
title: "The Math Behind 3D-RCAN: Convolutions, Residuals and Attention"
description: "A mathematical deep dive into the neural network architecture used for aberration correction in fluorescence microscopy"
author: "Camila Ramirez"
date: "2026-03-26"
categories: [theory, deep learning, math, linear algebra]
draft: false
---

## Introduction

The 3D-RCAN network might seem like a black box, but it is built from 
three elegant mathematical ideas that connect directly to linear algebra. 
In this post I break down each one.

## 1. Convolutions — Sliding Matrix Multiplication

A 3D convolution is the core operation in this network. Conceptually, 
we slide a small matrix called a **kernel** $W$ over the input volume $x$ 
and compute a weighted sum at each position:

$$y[i,j,k] = \sum_{p,q,r} W[p,q,r] \cdot x[i+p, j+q, k+r]$$

Where:
- $x$ is the input 3D image (a tensor of shape $Z \times Y \times X$)
- $W$ is the learned kernel (e.g. $3 \times 3 \times 3$)
- $y$ is the output feature map

Think of it as a dot product between the kernel and every local patch 
of the image. The network learns the values of $W$ during training.

In matrix notation for a single patch:

$$y = W \cdot x_{patch} + b$$

Where $b$ is a bias term. This is pure linear algebra.

## 2. Residual Learning — Learning the Difference

A key innovation in RCAN is **residual learning**. Instead of learning 
the full mapping from input to output directly, the network learns the 
*residual* — the difference between input and desired output.

A standard network learns:
$$y = F(x)$$

A residual network learns:
$$y = F(x) + x$$

The $+x$ term is called a **skip connection**. It passes the original 
input directly to the output and adds it to the learned transformation.

Why does this help? Because learning $F(x) = 0$ (no change needed) is 
much easier than learning $F(x) = x$ (identity mapping). The network 
only needs to learn corrections, not the full image.

In the 3D-RCAN architecture this is applied hierarchically — residuals 
within residuals, called **Residual-in-Residual** structure:

$$y_{group} = F_{group}(x) + x$$
$$y_{block} = F_{block}(x) + x$$

## 3. Channel Attention — Adaptive Feature Weighting

Not all feature channels are equally important. The **channel attention 
mechanism** learns to weight each channel adaptively.

For a feature map with $C$ channels, we first compute the global average 
of each channel (called Global Average Pooling):

$$z_c = \frac{1}{Z \cdot Y \cdot X} \sum_{i,j,k} x_c[i,j,k]$$

This gives us a vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^C$ — one number per channel 
summarizing its overall activation.

Then we pass it through two small linear layers with a bottleneck:

$$s = \sigma\left(W_2 \cdot \text{ReLU}(W_1 \cdot z)\right)$$

Where:
- $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{C/r \times C}$ reduces dimensions (r is reduction ratio)
- $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times C/r}$ restores dimensions
- $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function (outputs values between 0 and 1)

Finally we multiply each channel by its learned weight:

$$\hat{x}_c = s_c \cdot x_c$$

This tells the network: *pay more attention to channels that contain 
useful information, and suppress channels that don't.*

## 4. Putting It All Together: The RCAB Block

A **Residual Channel Attention Block (RCAB)** combines all three ideas:

$$\text{RCAB}(x) = x + \text{ChannelAttention}(\text{Conv}(\text{ReLU}(\text{Conv}(x))))$$

In words:
1. Apply two convolutions with ReLU activation
2. Apply channel attention to reweight features
3. Add the original input back (residual connection)

The full 3D-RCAN stacks multiple RCABs into **Residual Groups**, and 
multiple Residual Groups into the full network.

## 5. Training: How the Network Learns

The network is trained on pairs of images:
- **Input:** Aberrated microscopy image $\hat{x}$
- **Target:** Clean ground truth image $x^*$

It minimizes the **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} |f(\hat{x}_i) - x^*_i|$$

Where $f$ is the network and $N$ is the number of pixels. The network 
updates its weights using **gradient descent**:

$$W \leftarrow W - \alpha \nabla_W \mathcal{L}$$

Where $\alpha$ is the learning rate. This is the chain rule of calculus 
applied to matrices — exactly what you study in multivariable calculus 
and linear algebra!

## Connection to Your Classes

| Concept | Class |
|---------|-------|
| Matrix multiplication (convolutions) | Linear Algebra |
| Gradient descent, chain rule | Multivariable Calculus |
| Dot products, vector spaces | Linear Algebra |
| Eigenvalues (feature channels) | Linear Algebra |
| Loss minimization | Calculus |

## Summary

3D-RCAN is elegant because it builds a powerful image restoration system 
from simple mathematical operations you already know:
- **Convolution** = sliding dot products
- **Residual learning** = addition of matrices
- **Channel attention** = learned scaling of vectors
- **Training** = gradient descent on a loss function

The "deep learning" part is really just applying these operations 
millions of times with learned parameters.
